coredata.dc:title: A carbon dots based fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of hemoglobin
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Shan
Wang, Lumin
Huang, Chusheng
Xie, Jiangning
Su, Wei
Sheng, Jiarong
Xiao, Qi
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we presented a rapid, selective and highly sensitive probe for hemoglobin (HGB) based on the quenched fluorescence of carbon dots (CDs). The CDs were synthesized by microwave irradiation technique in one pot according to our previous report. The fluorescence of CDs was effectively quenched by HGB due to the ground state complex formation between CDs and HGB. A number of key factors including the amount of CDs, pH value of phosphate buffer solution, the concentration of salt solution, and the reaction time those influenced the analytical performance of CDs in HGB determination were investigated thoroughly. Under the experimental conditions, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was inversely proportional to the concentration of HGB ranging from 1 to 4000nM with a correlation coefficient of 0.9987. The limit of detection for HGB was as low as 0.12nM. Some common substances, including other heme proteins, did not affect HGB determination significantly. The method was utilized to detect HGB in human urine and human blood samples with satisfactory results. The static quenching mechanism of this system was further investigated by UV–vis absorption spectrometry and time-resolved fluorescence spectrometry. The α-helix content of HGB decreased and the secondary structure of HGB was rearranged after the interaction with CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent probe
Hemoglobin
Static quenching mechanism
Structure variation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic illustration of HGB determination using CDs fluorescent probe.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs (a) and HGB (d), the excitation spectrum of CDs at λ em =430nm (b) and the emission spectrum of CDs at λ ex =340nm (c). Insert: Photograph of CDs under the radiation of white light (left) and 365nm UV lamp (right). (B) Fluorescence spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelength. (C) FT-IR spectrum of CDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs in different concentrations of HGB (a–t): 0, 1, 3, 5, 7, 10, 50, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 700, 1000, 2000, 3000, 4000, 6000, 10,000, 20,000nM. (B) The dependence of fluorescence intensity of CDs on HGB concentrations. Insert: linear relationship between I 0/I and HGB concentration in the range of 1–4000nM. CDs: 10.7mgmL−1; phosphate buffer: pH 7.5.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, HGB and CDs-HGB system; the sum absorption spectrum between CDs and HGB, respectively. CDs: 10.7mgmL−1; HGB: 20μM. (B) Fluorescence decay traces of CDs and CDs-HGB system. All measurements were made at λ em =430nm. τ is the fluorescence decay time of CDs, and b is the normalized preexponential factor, respectively. CDs: 10.7mgmL−1; HGB: 20μM.
(A) FT-IR spectra of CDs, HGB, and the difference spectrum between CDs-HGB system and CDs. CDs: 10.7mgmL−1; HGB: 500μM. (B) CD spectra of CDs, HGB, and CDs-HGB system. CDs: 10.7mgmL−1; HGB: 4μM.
Comparison of methods for the determination of HGB.

row.entry: Ionic liquid (methylimidazolium chloride, NmimCl)-polyvinyl chloride ionomer (NmimCl-PVC)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effect of coexisting foreign substances for HGB determination. a
Detection of HGB in synthetic samples (n =5).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: 500μM CaCl2, 500μM MgCl2, 50μM FeSO4, 100μM ZnCl2, 200μM Pb(NO3)2, and 50μM CuSO4.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: 500μM ascorbic acid, 300μM oxidized glutathione, 300μM reduced glutathione, 50μM lysozyme, and 550μM trypsin.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: 500μM L-asparagine, 500μM L-arginine, 500μM L-threonine, 500μM L-phenylalanine, 500μM L-proline, and 500μM L-aspartic acid.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: 500μM L-tyrosine, 500μM L-glycine, 300μM L-glutamine, 500μM L-cysteine, 300μM L-serine, and 50μM L-methionine.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: e
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: 500μM L-valine, 500μM L-histidine, 500μM L-lysine, 500μM L-alanine, and 70μM L-glutamic acid.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: f
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: 500μM guanine, 500μM adenine, 6μM cytochrome C, 6μM protoporphyrin IX, and 6μM myglobin.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: g
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: 500μM cytosine, 500μM thymine, 10μM bovine serum albumin, and 10μM human serum albumin.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of HGB in human urine and human blood samples (n =5).

head.ce:title: A carbon dots based fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of hemoglobin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Guangxi Teachers Education University, Nanning 530001, P.R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Guangxi Teachers Education University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Center of Southwest Ethnic Medicine, Guangxi Normal University, Guilin, 541004, P.R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Southwest Ethnic Medicine, Guangxi Normal University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Guangxi Teachers Education University, Nanning 530001, P.R. China. Tel.: +86 771 3908065; fax: +86 771 3908065.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we presented a rapid, selective and highly sensitive probe for hemoglobin (HGB) based on the quenched fluorescence of carbon dots (CDs). The CDs were synthesized by microwave irradiation technique in one pot according to our previous report. The fluorescence of CDs was effectively quenched by HGB due to the ground state complex formation between CDs and HGB. A number of key factors including the amount of CDs, pH value of phosphate buffer solution, the concentration of salt solution, and the reaction time those influenced the analytical performance of CDs in HGB determination were investigated thoroughly. Under the experimental conditions, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was inversely proportional to the concentration of HGB ranging from 1 to 4000nM with a correlation coefficient of 0.9987. The limit of detection for HGB was as low as 0.12nM. Some common substances, including other heme proteins, did not affect HGB determination significantly. The method was utilized to detect HGB in human urine and human blood samples with satisfactory results. The static quenching mechanism of this system was further investigated by UV–vis absorption spectrometry and time-resolved fluorescence spectrometry. The α-helix content of HGB decreased and the secondary structure of HGB was rearranged after the interaction with CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of carbon nanomaterials with sizes of less than 10nm, have attracted tremendous attention in diverse research fields recently [1–3]. Compared with some conventional semiconductor quantum dots with heavy metal cores, CDs are superior in terms of low cytotoxicity, excellent aqueous solubility, good biocompatibility, broader photoluminescence profiles, and high photostability [4,5]. More attentions have been paid to CDs, including their synthesis and biochemical applications [6–8]. Recent studies based on the fluorescence variation of CDs in the detection process, CDs have been utilized to monitor many substances, such as Cu2+, Fe3+, methylmercury, and thrombin [9–12]. Vedamalai et al. have developed a novel luminescence sensing system for Cu2+ detection based on the fluorescence enhancement of CDs [9]. Chen's group have established N-doped CDs for sensitive and selective probing of Fe3+ in living cells [10]. Costas-Mora et al. reported a sensitive and selective detection of methylmercury based on the fluorescence quenching of CDs [11]. Wang et al. developed an aptamer biosensor for thrombin based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer from upconverting phosphors to CDs [12]. Although CDs are low cost, the detection process of thrombin is quite complicated. It is highly desirable to develop a simple detection process for such biomacromolecules.

Hemoglobin (HGB), which is the main protein component of red blood cells, is a tetrameric metalloprotein that composed of a globular protein part and four iron-containing heme parts. HGB plays a vital role in transporting molecular oxygen physiologically in mammalian blood from the respiratory organs to the different organs of the body, and its sophisticated function for oxygen transporting are fully illustrated previously [13–15]. The amount of HGB in blood is associated with many clinical disease, such as leukemia, anemia, heart diseases and so forth, while its normal level displays the well-functioning of the organism [16]. Therefore, the easy and accurate quantitative analysis of HGB in clinical diagnostics is essential to assess the extent of diseases and to evaluate various intervention programs aimed at control of diseases. Recently, numerous attempts have been made to detect HGB including colorimetric approaches [17,18], spectrophotometric methods [19–23], electrochemical measurements [24,25], and chemiluminescence determination [26]. Unfortunately, the majority of the aforementioned techniques suffer from the application of toxic reagents, expensive equipment, complicated sample pretreatment, and relatively higher detection limit of HGB. In spite of the recent improvement in the determination of HGB, development of simple, sensitive, efficient, cost-effective and user-friendly assay for trace amount of HGB detection is still highly demanded in clinical analysis and disease treatment.

Compared with the aforementioned measurements, limited investigation has been spend on the detection of HGB by using fluorescent CDs with fluorescence spectrometry, especially fluorescence quenching strategy. Due to the distinct advantages of easy operation, high sensitivity and selectivity, CDs-based fluorescent probe for the determination of HGB becomes a good alternative. Up to date, the reported fluorescent probe based on CDs for HGB is relatively few. Inspired by these facts, we fabricated a CDs-based fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive determination of HGB by simple mixing blue-emission fluorescent CDs and HGB in solution (Scheme 1 ). Herein, a facile method for the fabrication of fluorescent CDs through microwave irradiation technique in one pot was present. Influences of the amount of CDs, pH value of phosphate buffer solution, the concentration of salt solution, and the reaction time on the fluorescence quenching of CDs by HGB were investigated thoroughly. Based on fluorescence quenching strategy, selective and sensitive determination of trace amount of HGB using CDs as fluorescent probe was successfully performed. Importantly, the practical application of the developed strategy was demonstrated by employing this probe to assay HGB in human urine and human blood samples. Additionally, the quenching mechanism of CDs-HGB system and the conformation variation of HGB were discussed thoroughly. This method has been demonstrated label-free, highly sensitive and selective for practical on-site and visual determination of HGB.

Polyethylene glycol 1500 (PEG 1500), glycerine, and L-serine were purchased from Sigma (St. Louis, MO, USA). HGB, amino acids, nucleotides, and proteins were obtained from Sigma (St. Louis, MO, USA). All other reagents were of analytical-reagent grade and used as received. Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2MΩcm was produced by passing through a RiOs 8 unit followed by a Millipore-Q Academic purification set (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA) and used throughout the whole experiments.

Fluorescent CDs were prepared by using CEM Discover Benchmate microwave reactor (CEM, USA). UV–vis absorption spectra were measured on Cary 100 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Inc., Australia). Fluorescence spectra were recorded with RF-5301 PC luminescence spectrometer (Shimadzu Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). Time-resolved fluorescence decay traces were recorded with Fluorolog-3 system (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France) by using an excitation wavelength of 378nm. FT-IR spectra were recorded on Nicolet iS10 spectrometer (Thermo, USA). Circular dichroism (CD) spectra were recorded on Chirascan CD spectrometer (Applied Photophysics, England) using a quartz cells with 0.1cm path length. All pH measurements were made with a basic pH meter PB-10 (Sartorius Scientific Instruments Co., Ltd., Beijing, China).

Fluorescent CDs were synthesized by microwave irradiation technique in one pot according to a previous method after minor modification [27]. Briefly, 1.0g PEG 1500 and 15mL glycerine were mixed thoroughly, and the mixture was pretreated with microwave reactor under 200W and 160°C for 15min. After the mixed solution naturally cooled down to room temperature, 1.0g L-serine was added into the solution. Then, the mixture was further treated with microwave reactor under 200W and 180°C for 10min. The obtained mixture was cooled to room temperature again. The obtained CDs were purified by dialyzing against ultrapure water by using dialysis membranes with 1000 MWCO for 3 days. The CDs solution was then concentrated by rotary evaporator to 5mL and stored at 4°C before application.

For HGB determination, 2mL phosphate buffer (pH 7.5), 180μL 178.3mgmL−1 of CDs and the appropriate aliquot of HGB solution were transferred orderly into a 5mL Eppendorf tube. The mixture was stirred thoroughly and finally diluted to 3mL with ultrapure water. After 1min incubation at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra were measured. The fluorescence spectra were recorded under excitation wavelength of 340nm, and the band-slits of excitation and emission were set as 10.0 and 5.0nm, respectively. The sensitivity was fixed on high and the response time was set as 0.5s. The fluorescence spectra were recorded from 350 to 600nm, and the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 430nm was used for the quantitative analysis of HGB.

Human urine and human blood samples were obtained from healthy male and female adult volunteers in our laboratory. For human urine samples determination, human urine samples were centrifuged, and were then diluted with 100-fold ultrapure water prior to being determined. For human blood samples determination, 1mL human blood samples were centrifuged at 500rpm for 6min to get rid of serum. The reserved red blood cells were washed for three times with 0.9% physiological saline solution and then dissolved by toluene. After centrifugation at 2000rpm for 10min, human blood samples were diluted with 1000-fold ultrapure water before detection.

As shown in Fig. 1A, CDs exhibited broad absorption bands from 200 to 400nm in the typical UV–vis absorption spectrum. Like most fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, a typical absorption peak around 340nm, which was due to the n–π * electronic transition of CDs, was originated from the trapping of excited state energy by the surface states which results in strong fluorescence [28]. Meanwhile, the fluorescence spectra indicated that CDs exhibited a narrow fluorescence spectrum with a strong emission peak at 430nm under the excitation wavelength of 340nm. Furthermore, the CDs solution was pale yellow under ambient daylight but exhibited obvious blue emission under UV light irradiation (365nm), demonstrating the strong blue fluorescence of CDs observed by the naked eyes.

In addition, CDs exhibited the same excitation-dependent fluorescence property as reported before [28]. The excitation-dependent fluorescence property was ascribed to the different surface state emissive trap of CDs. As indicated in Fig. 1B, with the excitation wavelength increased from 320 to 370nm, the maximum emission peak position of CDs exhibited an obvious red-shift continuously, and the maximum fluorescence intensity was firstly increased and subsequently decreased. The results demonstrated that the maximum excitation wavelength and the maximum emission wavelength of CDs were at 340 and 430nm, respectively.

The existence of surface functional groups of CDs was studied using FT-IR spectrometry. As indicated in Fig. 1C, the absorption peak at 3382cm−1 was due to the stretching vibration of O–H bonds, whereas the absorption peaks at 2939, 1413, 671, and 557cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations and the bending vibrations of C–H bonds. The absorption peaks at 1109 and 1041cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of C–O–C bonds, and the absorption peak at 1645cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of CO bonds. These results revealed that CDs were equipped with oxygen-containing functional groups (–COOH and –OH groups), which was conducive to the bio-functionalizations and applications of CDs in the biological analysis.

Furthermore, the influence of pH value on the fluorescence property of CDs was investigated. The fluorescence intensity of CDs dispersion changed upon regulating pH value from 5.0 to 9.0, and the fluorescence emission peak of CDs was still at 430nm (Fig. S1), which indicated that the fluorescence characteristics of CDs depended on the pH value to some extent. The effect of the concentration of NaCl on the fluorescence property of CDs was also investigated. As shown in Fig. S2, the fluorescence intensity of CDs dispersion at 430nm decreased upon regulating NaCl concentration from 0 to 0.667M, suggesting the reduced photostability of CDs at the presence of NaCl solution with higher concentration. In addition, CDs exhibited good photostability at room temperature. Even after continuously excitation at 340nm for 150min, CDs exhibited negligible variation in fluorescence intensity (Fig. S3). The relative quantum yield of CDs was examined to be 12.1% under 340nm excitation in reference to quinine sulfate whose quantum yield was 54% in 0.1 H2SO4 solution under 360nm excitation (Fig. S4). These CDs were nearly spherical with excellent monodispersity, and the average sizes of CDs were around 5nm (Fig. S5).

Based on the above fluorescence properties of CDs, CDs was utilized as the fluorescent probe for direct determination of HGB. In order to enhance the sensitivity of CDs fluorescent probe for HGB detection, some important experimental parameters were optimized: (a) amount of CDs; (b) pH value of phosphate buffer solution; (c) concentration of salt solution; (d) reaction time. The respective data the results were shown in Figs. S6, S7, S8 and S9. The following optimal experimental conditions were found to give best result: (a) amount of CDs of 10.7mgmL−1; (b) pH value of 7.5; (c) solution without NaCl; (d) reaction time of 1min

Under the optimum experimental conditions, the fluorescence spectra of CDs with increasing concentrations of HGB from 0 to 20μM were measured to reflect the sensitivity of CDs fluorescent probe for HGB detection. As illustrated in Fig. 2A, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was regularly decreased after the addition of HGB, suggesting that the fluorescence quenching of CDs was mainly dependent on the concentration of HGB. Meanwhile, the emission peak position of CDs exhibited an obvious red-shift continuously from 430 to 451nm after the addition of HGB. Since HGB exhibited two typical absorption peaks at 280 and 405nm (Fig. 1A), and the absorption spectrum of HGB overlapped with the emission spectrum of CDs to some extent (wavelength shorter than 430nm), which subsequently resulted in an obvious red-shift of the emission peak position.

The change of fluorescence intensity of CDs with increasing concentrations of HGB from 0 to 20μM is exhibited in Fig. 2B. The fluorescence intensity variation of CDs was proportional to the concentration of HGB in the range of 1–4000nM, which was described by the classical Stern-Volmer equation of (I 0/I)−1=2.8×105 ×[Q]. The insert calibration curve in Fig. 2B showed a good linear regression equation with a correlation coefficient of 0.9987. The relative standard deviation for 100nM HGB was 0.1% (n =5). Based on three times standard deviation of 12 measurements of CDs without HGB, the limit of detection for HGB was calculated to be 0.12nM that was comparable to the most sensitive spectrophotometric approach recently reported (Table 1 ).

For the sake of the applicability of CDs fluorescent probe for HGB determination in biological samples, the influence of some chemical substances, amino acids, nucleotides, and proteins were investigated. If the interfering substances cause a relative error of less than ±5% in the fluorescence intensity of CDs, they are considered to have no interference with HGB determination. As illustrated in Table 2 , some chemical substances, amino acids, and nucleotides, did not pose any serious interference in the determination of 1.0μM HGB. In addition, the interfering effects of other heme proteins, such as cytochrome C, protoporphyrin IX, and myglobin, were also investigated. The results indicated that their effects on the detection of 1.0μM HGB were neglected. Since the concentrations of cytochrome C and myglobin in blood are much lower than the concentration of HGB [29–31], this strategy can be applied to HGB detection in blood samples.

To confirm the feasibility, CDs fluorescent probe was firstly utilized to detect HGB in synthetic samples which contained the above interfering substances. As indicated in Table 3 , the values found for these synthetic samples were identical with the spiked values and the recoveries were obtained from 94.6% to 103.2%. In addition, this approach was applied to the detection of HGB in human urine and human blood samples. As indicated in Table 4 , the values found for human urine and human blood samples were highly identical with the spiked values and the recoveries were obtained from 95.2% to 105.1%, which validated the reliability and practicality of this CDs fluorescent probe.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism usually contains inner filter effect, dynamic quenching, and static quenching [27]. As illustrated in Fig. 1A, the absorption spectrum of HGB did not overlap effectively with the excitation spectrum of CDs, and the absorption ability of HGB at 430nm irradiation was very weak, suggesting that HGB could neither shield the excitation light for CDs nor absorb the emission light of CDs. Hence, the quenching mechanism in CDs-HGB system was not the inner filter effect.

Furthermore, dynamic quenching and static quenching mechanism can be easily distinguished by careful examination of the absorption spectrum of fluorophore, since the absorption spectrum of fluorophore keeps constant during dynamic quenching process but changes during static quenching process [27]. For the sake of precise revelation of quenching mechanism in this system, UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, HGB, and CDs-HGB system were recorded. As shown in Fig. 3A, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs-HGB system and the sum absorption spectrum between CDs and HGB was not superposed within the experimental error, indicating the ground state complex formation between CDs and HGB. Meanwhile, compared with the sum absorption spectrum between CDs and HGB, the absorption spectrum of CDs-HGB system showed a tiny absorbance decrease at 403nm, implying the hypochromic effect after the formation of CDs-HGB complex. These results heralded the static quenching mechanism of CDs-HGB system.

Moreover, the quenching mechanism can be clarified directly by testing the fluorescence lifetime of fluorophore, because the fluorescence lifetime of fluorophore is almost constant in static quenching process but will be altered in dynamic quenching process [32]. Therefore, the fluorescence decay curves of CDs and CDs-HGB system were recorded to reveal the exact quenching mechanism. As shown in Fig. 3B, the data were well fitted with biexponential equation and the average fluorescence lifetime was given by the sum of the ∑b i τ i products [27,32]. As exhibited in Fig. 3B, two fluorescence decay components of CDs were τ 1 1.77ns (25.85%) and τ 2 8.10ns (74.15%), and the average fluorescence lifetime of CDs was about 6.46ns. After the addition of HGB, two fluorescence decay components of CDs increased slightly, τ 1 2.26ns (33.49%) and τ 2 8.66ns (66.51%), and the average fluorescence lifetime of CDs was about 6.51ns. The average fluorescence lifetime of CDs was almost constant in the absence or presence of HGB, indicating the ground state complex formation between CDs and HGB and the static quenching mechanism of CDs-HGB system.

Since the fluorescence quenching mechanism of this CDs-HGB system was the static quenching mechanism, the ground state complex formation between CDs and HGB was existed. It is widely reported that the binding of other ligands with protein will affect the structure of protein. In order to investigate the conformation variation of HGB after the binding with CDs, FT-IR spectrometry and CD spectrometry are provided to monitor the structure change of HGB.

FT-IR spectrometry is an effective approach to monitor the structure change of protein after the binding with other ligands [33,34]. In order to investigate the conformation variation of HGB after the binding with CDs, FT-IR absorption spectra of CDs, HGB, and the difference absorption spectrum between CDs-HGB system and CDs were recorded. As shown in Fig. 4A, the absorption peak at 3304cm−1 of the spectrum of HGB was ascribed to the stretching vibration of O–H bonds. After the interaction with CDs, the absorption peak position of O–H stretching vibration bands of HGB moved from 3304 to 3295cm−1, together with the increase of the peak intensity of O–H bonds, implying the enhancement of the hydrophilic property of HGB.

Moreover, the spectrum of HGB presented two typical acid amide I band (CO stretch) and acid amide II band (C–N stretch and N–H bending mode) centered at 1654 and 1543cm−1, respectively [33]. Plenty of detail information about secondary structure can be abstracted from the acid amide I band. The absorption of each secondary structure can be classified depending on the wavenumber of sub-peaks: 1610–1640cm–1 stands for the absorption of β-sheet structure; 1648–1655cm−1 stands for the absorption of α-helix structure; 1655–1660cm−1 stands for the absorption of random coil structure; 1660–1695cm−1 stands for the absorption of β-turn structure [34]. After the addition of CDs, the peak position of acid amide I band of HGB moved from 1654 to 1657cm−1, suggested that the binding interaction of CDs with HGB resulted in the reduction of the α-helix content and the rearrangement of the secondary structure of HGB.

CD spectrometry, which is another effective strategy to directly investigate the structure variation of protein after the interaction with other ligands, can provide the detail information about the percentages of different secondary structure, such as α-helix, β-sheet, β-turn, and random coil [35]. To prove the possible influence of CDs on the secondary structure of HGB, the CD spectra of CDs, HGB, and CDs-HGB system were performed. As shown in Fig. 4B, HGB exhibited two negative absorption peaks at 208 and 222nm which were the characteristic absorption peaks of the α-helix structure of proteins [35]. After the addition of CDs, the absorbance of two negative bands at 208 and 222nm all decreased significantly, indicating the secondary structure variation of HGB, especially the α-helix structure. In order to accurately quantify the α-helical structure of HGB, the results are expressed in mean residue ellipticity (MRE) (degcm2 dmol−1) by the following equation:
(1)
MRE =
θ
obs
10 n l
C P

Herein, θ obs represents the measured ellipticity, n is the total number of amino acids of HGB (574), l is the path length (0.1cm), and C p is the concentration of HGB, respectively. Therefore, the content of the α-helix structure of HGB can be calculated through the following equation:
(2)
α
-helix =
−
MRE
208
nm
− 4000
33,000 − 4000
× 100 %

The calculated results indicated that the α-helix structure of HGB decreased from 34.7% to 24.3%, when the concentration of CDs increased from 0 to 10.7mgmL−1. In order to quantify the different contents of the secondary structures of HGB, CD spectra were further analyzed by the algorithm SELCON3. The values of secondary structures of HGB were found to be 34.7% (α-helix), 12.8% (β-sheet), 22.2% (β-turn), and 30.3% (random coil). After the addition of CDs, the values of secondary structures of HGB were found to be 24.3% (α-helix), 15.2% (β-sheet), 25.4% (β-turn), and 35.1% (random coil). An obvious decreasing tendency of the α-helix content and increasing tendencies of other secondary structure contents were estimated at the present of CDs, which indicated that the destruction of the hydrogen bonding networks and the decrease of the biological activity of HGB after the interaction with CDs.

In this paper, we developed a novel, simple, sensitive and selective fluorescent sensing method for the detection of HGB by using CDs as fluorescent probe. The fluorescent probe needs no expensive materials and provides a simple and high reproducible assay. The fluorescence of CDs was quenched by HGB through static quenching mode. Under the optimum conditions, the limit of detection of HGB was as low as 0.12nM. The quenching process between CDs and HGB was mainly based on the static quenching mechanism, therefore the probe was selective against most of the common interferences, including other heme proteins. The presented method was applied to the detection of HGB in synthetic samples, human urine and human blood samples successfully. The static quenching mechanism was further investigated by UV–vis absorption spectrometry and time-resolved fluorescence spectrometry. The conformation variation of HGB was investigated by FT-IR spectrometry and CD spectrometry. After the binding interaction with CDs, the secondary structure of HGB was rearranged and the biological activity of HGB was subsequently decreased.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21203035, 21403039), the Guangxi Natural Science Foundation (2013GXNSFCA019005), the Scientific Research Foundation of Guangxi Provincial Education Department (ZD2014081), the Innovation Project of Guangxi Graduate Education (YCSZ2015187), Collaborative Innovation Center of Southwest Ethnic Medicine (Guangxi Normal University, CICSEM 2013-B6), and Guangxi Colleges and Universities Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecular Chemistry, Guangxi Teachers Education University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2015.07.099.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots for multiphoton bioimaging
J. Am. Chem. Soc.
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging
Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.
Red, green, and blue luminescence by carbon dots: full-color emission tuning and multicolor cellular imaging
Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.
Solar hydrogen production using carbon quantum dots and a molecular nickel catalyst
J. Am. Chem. Soc.
Room-temperature phosphorescence logic gates developed from nucleic acid functionalized carbon dots and graphene oxide
Nanoscale
Tuning laccase catalytic activity with phosphate functionalized carbon dots by visible light
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces
One pot synthesis of highly luminescent polyethylene glycol anchored carbon dots functionalized with a nuclear localization signal peptide for cell nucleus imaging
Nanoscale
Facile and eco-friendly synthesis of green fluorescent carbon nanodots for applications in bioimaging, patterning and staining
Nanoscale
Carbon nanodots prepared from o-phenylenediamine for sensing of Cu2+ ions in cells
Nanoscale
Solid-phase synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sensitive and selective probing ferric ions in living cells
Anal. Chem.
In situ building of a nanoprobe based on fluorescent carbon dots for methylmercury detection
Anal. Chem.
Aptamer biosensor based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer from upconverting phosphors to carbon nanoparticles for thrombin detection in human plasma
Anal. Chem.
Synthetic models for the oxygen-binding hemoproteins
Acc. Chem. Res.
Stereochemistry of cooperative effects in haemoglobin: haem–haem interaction and the problem of allostery
Nature
Use of dual wavelength spectrophotometry and continuous enzymatic depletion of oxygen for determination of the oxygen binding constants of hemoglobin
Anal. Biochem.
Facile synthesis of N-acetyl-L-cysteine capped CdHgSe quantum dots and selective determination of hemoglobin
Spectrochim. Acta A
G-quadruplex DNAzymes-induced highly selective and sensitive colorimetric sensing of free heme in rat brain
Analyst

RSC Adv.
Fluorescence enhancement of imidazolium ionic liquid by its confinement on PVC for in situ selective quantification of hemoglobin
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces
A novel fluorescence assay for the detection of hemoglobin based on the G-quadruplex/hemin complex
Talanta

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Shan Huang received her Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Wuhan University in 2009. After a two-year postdoctoral (2009–2011) stay at the Institut Armand-Frappier, Institut National de la Recherche Scientifique (INRS) and INRS-Énergie, Matériaux et Télécommunications, Université du Québec, Canada, she returned to China as an associate professor at the Guangxi Teachers Education University. Her current research interests are the synthesis of functional nanomaterials and application in biochemical sensors.
Lumin Wang is pursuing her master degree in analytical chemistry at Guangxi Teachers Education University. She works on the development of novel nanomaterials for fluorescent sensors.

Chusheng Huang received his Ph.D. degree in organic chemistry from Lanzhou University in 1998. Now, he is a professor at Guangxi Teachers Education University. His current research interests are the synthesis of natural products and their application in chemical sensors.
Jiangning Xie is pursuing his master degree in analytical chemistry at Guangxi Teachers Education University. He works on the synthesis of functional nanomaterials.

Wei Su received his Ph.D. degree in inorganic chemistry from Shandong University in 2007. Now, he is a professor at Guangxi Teachers Education University. His current research interests are the synthesis of functional ruthenium anticancer drugs and their interactions with DNA.

Jiarong Sheng received his master degree in organic chemistry from Guangxi Normal University in 1993. Now, she is a professor at Guangxi Teachers Education University. Her current research interests are the synthesis of Se-based anticancer drugs and their application in clinic chemistry.

Qi Xiao received his Ph.D. degree in physical chemistry from Wuhan University in 2010. After a one-year postdoctoral (2010–2011) stay at the University of Massachusetts, USA, he returned to China as a professor at Guangxi Teachers Education University. His current research interests are the synthesis of functional graphene nanomaterials and their application in electrochemical sensors.
